title: Amazon Ownership: tell me about a time you took on something outside your defined role.

transcript.text: Amazon Ownership: tell me about a time you took on something outside your defined role.

blocks.md:
## "A time you took on something outside your defined role" — Amazon, Ownership

Story: W6 — Pepsi supplier self-service debugging · Amazon Ownership · ~100s. Backup is W7 (DSD associate notifications) if they want another.

### Situation
I'm a backend engineer on the supplier data APIs at Walmart. My actual job that quarter was building endpoints. Nobody asked me to look at how suppliers debugged their own data. But I kept seeing Pepsi in our support queue — same account, over and over.

### Task
When I dug in, one Pepsi debugging session was 50-plus repeated re-queries, all round-tripping through Walmart support. That wasn't a ticket assigned to me. I just decided it was my problem to fix.

### Action
First I went and watched what they were actually doing. They had zero direct visibility into their own API audit data, so every "why did my request fail?" became a support email and a two-day wait.

The audit data was already sitting in GCS as Parquet files. So I built BigQuery external tables right on top of it — meaning suppliers could run plain SQL against their own data, no pipeline, no copy.

The hard part was isolation. Pepsi can't see Coca-Cola's rows. I enforced that with row-level security — a policy tag on `supplier_id` — so each supplier only ever sees their own data, even though it's one shared table.

> 💬 Layman version of that: "It's one big table for everyone, but each supplier has a key that only unlocks their own rows. So they self-serve safely without ever touching anyone else's data."

### Result
A debug that used to take two days now takes about 30 seconds. It freed up roughly 12 hours a week of our support time. And it changed how I see my role — the API isn't done when it returns 200, it's done when the supplier can actually operate it without us.

**Technical depth — if they probe:**
- BigQuery external tables = query the Parquet files in GCS directly, no ingestion step or second copy of the data.
- Row-level security via policy tag on `supplier_id` — access control lives on the column, enforced at query time.
- The audit data was already there because of the shared audit library (W2) writing to GCS → BigQuery; I reused that pipeline instead of building new storage.
- Parquet is columnar, so supplier filter-and-scan queries stay cheap even at scale.

**Likely follow-ups:**
- "Was this approved / how'd you get buy-in?" → I prototyped it first on existing data, showed the support-hours number, then took it to the team. Easy yes once the cost was visible.
- "How do you guarantee a supplier can't see another's data?" → Row-level policy tag, tested with a cross-supplier query that correctly returned zero rows.
- "Why not a dashboard or a new API?" → Suppliers already knew SQL and wanted ad-hoc queries; external tables were days of work, not weeks.
- "Did this scale to other suppliers?" → Yes, it's the same table — onboarding a new supplier is just their policy tag, no new build.

**What NOT to say:**
- Don't say "it wasn't my job so I waited for permission" — the whole point is you moved first.
- Don't bury the customer impact under the tech. Lead with "noticed a customer in pain."
- Don't claim you rebuilt the storage — you reused the existing audit pipeline. Be honest about that; it's frugal, which is also Amazon.

🎙️ **Say-it script:**
"So I'm a backend engineer on the supplier data APIs, and my job that quarter was building endpoints. But I kept noticing Pepsi in our support queue — same account, over and over. When I dug in, one debugging session was 50-plus repeated re-queries, every one of them going through Walmart support. Nobody assigned me that. I just decided it was mine to fix.

I went and watched what they did. They had no direct visibility into their own API data, so every 'why did this fail?' became a support email and a two-day wait. The audit data was already sitting in GCS as Parquet. So I built BigQuery external tables right on top of it — suppliers could run their own SQL, no new pipeline. The tricky part was isolation: Pepsi can't see Coca-Cola's rows. I handled that with row-level security, a policy tag on supplier_id, so each one only sees their own data.

A debug that took two days now takes about 30 seconds. Saved roughly 12 hours a week of support. And honestly it changed how I think — the API isn't done when it returns 200, it's done when the customer can actually run it themselves."

💬 **60-second version:**
"As a backend engineer, I noticed one supplier — Pepsi — was making 50-plus re-queries per debug, all through our support team. Nobody assigned me that. The audit data was already in GCS as Parquet, so I built BigQuery external tables on top with row-level security on supplier_id, so each supplier self-serves their own data safely. Debug time went from two days to 30 seconds, and saved about 12 hours a week of support. I took it on because the job isn't done when the API returns 200 — it's done when the customer can operate it."

Want me to run the **W7 DSD** story as a second Ownership example, or take one of those follow-ups?
